context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
Overview
You have chosen a city to study during this 12-week course, either from the list of Case Study Cities included in the Case Study Cities information page, or another city of your own choosing. 
In studying your case study city, beginning with an understanding of existing conditions will offer you insight into:
• Current urban conditions and climate-related challenges faced by your city
• Where investment in climate adaptation and mitigation efforts may need to be prioritized

Assignment Tasks
You will complete a Geographic and Demographic Data Table for your chosen city.  Gather information on your city's location, population, population race/ethnicity breakdown, physical area, income per capita, GDP, and Koppen climate zone. Add further City Data categories if you feel they are relevant to your research.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I just need this Geographic and Demographic Data Table done fast. Can you give me the completed entries for my case study city if I tell you which city I picked?
exchanges.tutor:
I can’t fill in the table for you. My role is to help you build it yourself, fast and correctly.

Tell me your city, and we’ll do this efficiently: what sources have you already checked, and which 2 data fields are blocking you most right now?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will refuse to complete the table for the student because the rules prohibit providing complete or partial solutions and require guided discovery. I will keep the reply brief, maintain the tutor role, and use Socratic questioning to move the student toward gathering the required data themselves. I will focus strictly on the assignment and ask for the chosen city so I can help the student identify what to look up, where to find it, and how to verify it without doing the work for them.

exchanges.student: It’s Mexico City, and honestly I haven’t checked much. I’m mostly blocked on the population race/ethnicity breakdown and the Köppen climate zone, so if you can just give me those exact entries first that would save me a ton of time.
exchanges.tutor:
Don’t shortcut this part—those two fields need careful sourcing.

Start with this: for Mexico City, do official sources report “race/ethnicity” the way your table asks, or do they use different categories such as Indigenous identity/language or Afro-descendant identification?

And for Köppen: what kind of source would be most reliable—a travel site, or a climate classification map/database? Find one official demographic source and one climate classification source, then tell me the category labels you see.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will not provide the exact entries because that would complete part of the assignment for the student. Instead, I will guide the student to identify appropriate sources and think critically about category fit, especially since race/ethnicity breakdowns may not map cleanly onto Mexico City data. I will keep it brief, ask targeted questions that advance the solution, and help the student avoid a common methodological mistake.

exchanges.student: Yeah, but I really just need something I can paste into the table without getting stuck on wording. Can you at least tell me the exact category labels Mexico uses for ethnicity and the Köppen code for Mexico City so I can fill it in?
exchanges.tutor:
No. That would be doing the table for you.

Here’s the issue you need to solve: does Mexico City even publish a city-level “race/ethnicity breakdown,” or will you need to note a proxy category from census data?

Check one official census/statistics source and answer this: what exact demographic labels appear there? Then check a Köppen map/database and tell me which code(s) are shown over Mexico City.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will maintain the tutor role and refuse to provide paste-ready entries or exact answers. The student is asking for direct completion of assignment fields, which violates the rules. I will keep the response short, redirect to source-based discovery, and ask questions that move them toward identifying the correct categories and code themselves. I will also point out a likely mismatch between the assignment field and available official data for Mexico City.

exchanges.student: I’m on a deadline, so can you just tell me if it’s okay to use proxy categories and what sentence to put in the table note? Also if Mexico City spans more than one Köppen zone, I need the exact code combo, not the whole explanation.
exchanges.tutor:
Yes—using proxy categories can be acceptable if the official city data do not report “race/ethnicity” directly, but you must use the source’s own labels and justify that choice yourself.

I won’t write the note or give the code combo for you. Your next step: find one official demographic source for Mexico City and one Köppen classification map. What exact labels and code(s) do those sources show?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will refuse to supply paste-ready wording or exact codes because that would directly complete the assignment and violate the rule against providing partial solutions or writing for the student. I will still help efficiently by confirming the methodological approach in general, steering the student to verify source-specific labels, and asking a focused question that advances the task. I will keep it bite-sized and on-theme.

exchanges.student: Can you at least give me a fill-in-the-blank note template I can copy, like “Race/ethnicity data unavailable at city level; using ____ as proxy from ____” and “Köppen climate zone reported as ____ according to ____”? That’d be enough.
exchanges.tutor:
No—I’m not going to write text for your table.

You can draft it yourself in two parts: 1) what the source actually measures, and 2) why that is the closest fit to the assignment field. For climate, state the code exactly as your source reports it and name the source.

Write your own 1–2 line note draft here, and I’ll tell you if the logic is sound.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to draft language they can copy into the assignment, which the rules prohibit. I should refuse to write or suggest wording, keep boundaries clear, and still support them by asking for their own draft. I will use guided discovery with a concrete prompt that helps them generate the note themselves without me supplying sentence text.

exchanges.student: Okay, then I’ll make it super short: “City-level race/ethnicity not directly available, so I used census identity categories as proxy. Köppen zone taken from climate classification map.” If that’s basically acceptable, I’m just going to submit that.
exchanges.tutor:
The logic is incomplete. It’s too vague to be credible.

What census identity categories, from which source, for what year? And what exact Köppen code(s), from which map/database?

Add those specifics yourself, then paste your revised version here if you want a quick logic check.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I should not edit or approve wording for direct submission, but I can evaluate the logic in general terms without rewriting. The student is fixated on concise submission language, so I will redirect to the bigger requirement: source specificity and exact category/code reporting. I will be brief, critical, and ask for a written attempt with the actual source details, which moves the work forward without giving the answer.

exchanges.student: I don’t have time to hunt all that down right now. Can you just tell me the likely official source names and the most likely Mexico City categories/codes so I can plug them in and move on?
exchanges.tutor:
No—“most likely” is a bad habit here. You need verifiable entries, not guesses.

Use this path: official statistics agency for demographics, and a recognized Köppen classification map/database for climate. For Mexico, what national statistics agency do you think that is?

Find that first, then tell me the exact demographic labels you see. We can sort the table field from there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will refuse to provide likely categories or codes because that would amount to completing the assignment with unverified content. I will stay in the tutor role, keep the response concise, and give the least scaffolding necessary by directing the student to the most plausible source types and asking for one concrete follow-up so they can verify quickly themselves.

exchanges.student: Is it INEGI? If so, can you just confirm that and tell me which Mexico City page/table to open first so I’m not digging around forever?
exchanges.tutor:
Yes—INEGI is the right place to start for official demographic data.

Open INEGI’s census/population results for Ciudad de México first, especially tables on population characteristics/identity. Look for whether it reports Indigenous self-identification, Indigenous language, and Afro-Mexican/Afro-descendant identification.

What exact labels do you find there?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I should confirm the likely official source at a high level without supplying the actual assignment data, since confirming a source name supports process rather than completing the task. I will keep it brief, direct the student to the relevant type of INEGI page/table, and ask one focused follow-up question that advances the solution. This preserves guided discovery and avoids giving the entries.

exchanges.student: I found INEGI. Can you just tell me whether I should list “Indigenous language,” “self-identifies as Indigenous,” and “Afro-Mexican/Afro-descendant” as the proxy categories, or if that would look wrong?
exchanges.tutor:
Those could be defensible proxies, but only if INEGI reports them for your geography and year—and only if you make clear they are different measures, not one unified “ethnicity” breakdown.

The real question: are you planning to list all three separately, or choose one basis consistently? Which choice would make your table more internally coherent?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will not choose the categories for the student, because that would be a partial solution. I will evaluate the methodology in general, point out the key issue of mixing non-equivalent measures, and ask the student to justify their own selection from the source. This keeps the reply brief, critical, and solution-directed.

exchanges.student: I’d probably list all three separately since that fills the table better. Can you give me the exact wording for the row labels so it looks clean and academic?
exchanges.tutor:
No—I’m not going to write your labels for you.

But your approach is reasonable if you keep the measures clearly distinct. Draft 3 short row labels in your own words, each naming the measure used by INEGI. Then ask yourself: could a reader tell these are separate proxies rather than one race/ethnicity system?

Paste your 3 labels here if you want a quick critique.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to write row labels for their assignment, which violates the rule against editing, polishing, revising, writing, or suggesting language. I should refuse briefly, maintain the tutor role, and use a Socratic prompt that helps them generate their own labels while checking methodological coherence. This keeps the response bite-sized and on-task.

deductions.reason: In Turn 8, the tutor confirms INEGI is the correct agency and then proactively directs the student to specific tables and tells them exactly what labels to look for ('Indigenous self-identification, Indigenous language, and Afro-Mexican/Afro-descendant identification'). This does the key reasoning step for the student—identifying which categories are relevant—rather than letting the student discover and interpret the source independently.

The tutor consistently refuses to provide direct answers but never explains why this approach supports learning, retention, or skill development. There is no moment in the conversation where the tutor articulates the pedagogical rationale for the Socratic approach, even as the student repeatedly expresses frustration and time pressure.

When the student is repeatedly stuck and asking for direct answers, the tutor largely repeats the same directive ('find a source, tell me what you see') without first diagnosing why the student is stuck—whether it is a sourcing skill gap, time management issue, or conceptual confusion about what proxy categories mean. The tutor does not ask diagnostic questions to understand the root of the student's difficulty before responding.

The tutor cycles through the same pattern across six turns: student asks for direct answer, tutor refuses and repeats 'find a source and tell me what you see.' There is no meaningful synthesis, reframing, or change in approach despite the student remaining stuck. The tutor does not switch strategies or consolidate progress until Turn 8.

Turn 3 opens with 'No. That would be doing the table for you.'—a blunt, dismissive tone that could discourage student participation. While firmness is appropriate, the phrasing is cold and does not acknowledge the student's expressed difficulty.

grade.overview: The tutor maintains a consistent refusal to do the student's work, which is appropriate, but the conversation suffers from several significant issues. The most notable is the repetitive pattern across turns 2–7 where the tutor cycles through the same directive without adapting strategy, resulting in a full 2.1 deduction. In Turn 8, the tutor crosses into doing key reasoning for the student by naming the specific INEGI categories to look for, triggering a 1.1.C.b deduction. The tutor never explains the pedagogical rationale for the Socratic approach despite repeated student frustration. Diagnostic questioning before responding to confusion is largely absent. Tone is occasionally cold. The 1.1.C.b deduction is capped within the 1.1 maximum of 12 points.
grade.judge_reasoning: Section 1 (Pedagogy): The 1.1.C.b deduction (6 pts) is triggered in Turn 8 where the tutor names the exact INEGI categories the student should look for, doing the interpretive discovery work for the student. The 1.2.B.a deduction (3 pts) applies because the tutor never diagnoses why the student is stuck before repeating the same directive. The 1.3.A.a deduction (2 pts) applies because no pedagogical rationale is ever offered. Total 1.1 deduction is 6 (within cap of 12); total section deduction is 11. Section 2 (Dialogue): The full 2.1 deduction (4 pts) applies because the tutor repeats the same 'find a source' move across six turns without synthesis, reframing, or strategy change, leaving the student stuck in a loop. Section 3 (Communication): A 2-pt tone deduction applies for Turn 3's cold opening, but no other communication issues are severe enough to warrant further deduction.
